Yeah, for sure.
So we're a high growth agency, so we've got a lot of good examples that I could pull from.
I think two that are very public that I could speak to.
One, this is probably, like, my favorite case study that I worked on, just because it was actually so, so simple.
So we worked with...
A few years ago, I know if you're familiar with like the meditation app.
I use it all the time if I just want like some white noise to sleep too.
Yeah, but they also do like, I think like Kim Kardashian or Klee Kardashian, like had a series where they like, you know, speak softly in your ear and stuff like that.
We worked with them.
It was actually only a six month engagement, so we didn't have a lot of time.
But we were able to drive so much impact by the end of the engagement.
They were like, we want to keep working with you, but we're like three years ahead of where we thought we would be right now.
So we actually need to go back and figure out our, like our sales funnel because we're getting too many, too many leads.
The levers we pulled were really, really simple.
For them, was a primarily editorial in engagement.
And the biggest lever was that like they had their blog on a subdomain.
So they had a blog with a bunch of good content already.
Their primary domain had a really good brand, but it was mostly connected just with the app.
So the initial lever we just pulled was just migrate the blog.
And we saw a bunch of impact from that, but we also like implemented a lot of internal links.
Thank
To make sure that Google was actually able to find and crawl our content well, that really just scaled the content that was working.
We went from like, I think like five or 10 articles per month that we were publishing 50 articles per month with them because we were just seeing so much of the return.
That was a unique case for me where like the path to success was really clear and the levers we pulled ended up working really, really well.

The like the thing that, you know, internally we were kind of like, well, we kind of moved a little bit too fast here and, you know, didn't set them up to, you know, renew with us because of how fast we were moving.
But it was kind of like a flip-sided coin, you know, kind of there.
A little bit more probably it's like what we would see or what I see more often in engagement is I've been working with Webflow for three years now.

And so in order to get every like graphite is so in Marcel's analogy where he's like for consulting agencies, like you can either pay crap and get crap or you can pay a lot and then you still kind of get a mixed bag.
Like we're definitely on the end of.
And people are paying a lot, although we do really take a lot of pride and make sure we're giving premium work and driving results.
So I preface that to say working with three years with a client is really, really impressive in my opinion.
We have to drive two, three X ROI for it even to be a conversation of like, why is this SEO agency the biggest thing on my marketing budget type of deal?
So what we've, and Webflow has been really, really cool because they're a decently large org where they've got different stakeholders.
We've had to navigate different relationships.
So the engagement has really evolved over time.
So when we started, we were really siloed pretty much to editorial because, and Graphite's like a fully, I know you guys do a lot of editorial.
Graphite is definitely fully encompassing where we're doing programmatic, CRO, landing page optimization.
So we were really siloed to editorial just because we didn't have relationships with the appropriate stakeholders.
And I'll.
Our SEO point of contact at the time just wasn't interested in cross-collaboration.
So we spent probably eight months of the engagement, kicking off editorial, getting that running, but just kind of banging our head against the wall, trying to get stuff done elsewhere.
And then eventually we had to get our graphite's leadership involved.
And at the time I was an account manager, so I wasn't quite as equipped as a director as I would be to move those blockers now.
But eventually we got a leadership involved, we able to get a point of contact with leadership on the engineering side of Webflow.
And actually that was able to get us some headway into at least getting us some engineering resources.

And what we were able to align on was, what actually didn't really get us resources, what we were able to align on is, hey, we don't have resources to give you, but we can give you your own Webflow platform to work in and you guys can build stuff yourself and then we'll launch it.
So we spent the next probably six or eight months building our own things and launching them while editorial really turned into a content engine, which was really, really.
Impressive.

By year two, we were able to drive 2.5x ROI, and that was primarily through editorial because everything that we were standing up outside of that was going from zero to one, so it was taking some time.
And Webflow just being such a large domain, we couldn't really drive, like driving incremental impact just isn't showing up.
We had to really make sure we were taking big swings.
Then they had a full org restructure where our POC left, there came in a new director of marketing, and we actually used that as an opportunity to solve all the gaps that we didn't have before.
So we were able to build a relationship with this new director of marketing.

We emphasized the importance of cross-collaboration with them, and our new SEO POC built relationships with some other marketing and product teams, and we were able to make headway on, like, they have a huge template marketplace, and they do a huge showcase where people who, like, build their websites in Webflow can actually showcase them.

On Webflow, and those are two of their biggest conversion and traffic drivers, so we were actually able to make headway there as well, and that's what drove us really into the second, or to the third year of the engagement, where we're able to, like, drive really, really big business impact by impacting these important site sections.
And then they've been awesome, where we've been leaning in full steam to answer engine optimization, or geo, whatever you guys call it.
And we've actually seen the impact.
Since January, it's, like, exploded for us, where, so over, like, the past year, we've started to shift our content down the funnel.
We started tracking prompts instead of just topics, or, like, questions on top of our topics.

When we're creating content briefs, we're actually looking at AI overviews and chat GPT to, like, look at what type of information is in there and optimizing from that, just like we would competitors on Google.
And we've really seen the benefits from it, I think, in, like, July or August, almost 40 years.
We're not almost over 40% of our non-brand signups were coming from AI search and like 97% of that ChatGPT.
So they're definitely a unique client where for whatever reason their user base has adopted ChatGPT, you know, pretty much fully.
But we're also seeing the work that we're doing and we've done a lot of A-B testing where we, because we just know like based on adoption, like the chart's going to go up and to the right.
So we've done a lot of controlled tests where we actually know that the work that we're doing is driving impact as well.
So that's kind of like long term, I guess.
don't even know if I fully answered your question.

would say one caveat is we're counting everything from AI search as non-brand, just because there's no way to really delineate between the two.

How about, what would you frame as something as like your, maybe in some web flow is like, or just like more broadly in terms of like serving your customers, what you would see as like one of your biggest challenges or failures in your time there?
Challenges in general, I think, are understanding the blockers that like we don't have visibility into.
So like, so I just kicked off with a new client like three weeks ago, and it's, it's a very large org.
And we're quickly finding out like the people we kicked off the project with are not the people that we need to be speaking to.
I went through, I went through this very recently.
Oh man, that's always a fun journey.
And that is, is always a challenge, especially at the speed we need to move at in order to drive impact and show a return.
Yeah.
So that's the biggest, I feel like I'm pretty equipped to do it now where it's more of a challenge.

I will say in the past, it probably has been a failure where even in the sense where maybe the org isn't extremely complicated, but just like I'm trying to push something forward with the person and they keep telling me, giving me pushback.
And I'm not really understanding why.
I'm not realizing, well, it's because it's coming from their boss or they're fed up with engineering who isn't giving them the resources.
And then I didn't like take the steps to talk to the right person or empathize with their issue and find a solution together, just kind of moved on to the next thing.
One thing that was a curve, so I guess I'll just give you two things.
One other thing that was a curve for me, so I started as an account manager at Graphite three years ago and I've worked up to the director role.
So one thing that was a curve for me was I had to realize I had internal clients as well.
So I would be so focused on my account work and alignment.
As a team, like whoever we're working on accounts, sometimes we forget to align with leadership.
And it's not that leadership wasn't going to sign off on our plan or something.
It was just like getting everybody into the room and saying, yes, we all agree on this.
So there's never a perception that we're not all in it together or somebody's going rogue.
I've definitely had a situation where, you know, accounts don't always go to plan.
And when they don't, you want to make sure everybody is bought into the plan so that even if the outcome isn't what we wanted, we all feel like we took the correct steps.
So I think just that broader internal alignment.
And now I'm in a position where the internal clients above me are very few.
It's still important.
I have to, you know, my CEO will definitely just look at charts sometimes and will pop and be like, hey, what's going on?
So, you know, I need to be equipped for that.
But now it's more sort of like downward.
How do I make sure we actually are all aligned on the plan and that we all feel like we're taking the next steps and that nobody on my team.
Team is going rogue or doing things that we don't feel aligned on.
Yep, that makes sense.
Yeah.
Interesting challenge for sure.
Very much can relate to the stakeholder part.
I went through that.
One that had a few different twists and turns.
It was like, eventually I met someone and I was like, man, I cannot believe I haven't met you up until this point.
Six weeks into this, I really should have been talking to you earlier.

You know, these things start somewhere, not all the right people are brought in.
So, yeah, we definitely see there's the larger the org, there's more opportunity for misalignment, lack of communication.
I mean, one of the biggest gaps I think we're going to be solving is just like kind of playing a PM role, helping the SEO team engage with.

The engineer and get the resources and roadmapping they need and communicate that to leadership so they know the impact we're trying to get and that, you know, there's valid reasons for the resources we're asking for.
And that's a little bit different than like working with a client that doesn't have an SEO team or just has like one lead and you're kind of working directly with them.
So, yeah, it's a challenge for sure, but it's kind of fun.
It's like it's a puzzle and it's a different problem to solve for, you know, every client.
Yeah, yeah, I agree.
That's cool.

For you, for your clients, as you're thinking about, you know, building strategies that generate returns in the short term, but are keeping in mind that long term growth that inevitably they're going to want to hold you to.
How do you, how do you balance that?
Walk me through your, your thought process and approach.
Yeah, so I have a pretty aggressive roadmap for like the third, first, first 30 days with the goal to show impact.
In the first 60 to 90 days.

So, and it would be different, like if I was, you know, hopping into a client midstream, but say I'm kicking off like a new client, we're, you know, doing really, we're doing audits, both technically, and then on the site as well, to find opportunities for quick wins.
So, technically, we're making sure the right pages are indexed.
Do they have anything like, you know, a blog on a subdomain that we can quickly move that over?
Or do they have a bunch of crap that we don't want indexed?
Or do they not have the proper pages indexed?
That's the biggest thing.
Internal links are, are really, have been really impactful for us.
We do a diagnostic that we basically look at all the pages that they get and put them into buckets based on how many internal links they have, and then see how much traffic they get based on that.
Almost every client I've, we've run this diagnostic for the, the chart is a U shape where it's like.

One, two, three, four, five internal links get this much, then we go to six, seven, eight, and it's the watershed, you know, might be seven or eight or nine or 10, depending on the client, but it's always right around there.

So then we do our best to make sure all of our priority pages have at least that many internal links pointing to them, whether it's through a related article module, you know, strategic inline linking, you know, whatever it is.
We also look on page, those are two of the biggest technical quick wins.
Obviously, there's rendering issues, like there's something we want to fix immediately.
So I'm really focused on, can Google find our content, can Google index our content, and can Google service it actually in search?
And then we obviously, those same principles apply to AEO as well.
Then we're looking on site.
Some of the biggest wins we can take are just tweaking title tags and headers on the pages, especially for our really important landing pages.

So we'll try to, like, have conversations with the client to understand immediately, like, what are the pages, either get the data or just have the conversations, or what are the pages that are driving the most value for you now in terms of conversions?
And then we'll also look at the pages that are driving the most clicks, too, and we'll usually optimize both.
Right off the bat, we're not doing, like, wholesale, you we want to change the template and everything just because I don't want to cause a bunch of friction.

But just making sure we're speaking to the correct audience with the correct topics and the correct keywords in our title tags and headers, that's something we see Google recognize in a few days and get impact, you know, within, again, a few days or a couple weeks.
So that's, I think that's, that kind of, like, covers, like, what we get, like, really quick wins.

And then while doing that, we're trying to move as fast as we can with standing up whatever new content, whether it's new landing pages, new programmatic pages, new editorial content, we're having that alignment phase at, like, rapid speed, so we can get those pages live and start to validate that.
That as well, and move to where we can scale efficiently.

So I'm always looking at, like, what are the, and luckily I've just been doing this long enough where I know a lot of the quick levers that we can take, that we can pull, even though, you know, the storyline is SEO takes forever.
That's, it doesn't always need to be that way.
It's true.
Yeah.
It's usually, and then, and then, like, as we're doing that, we're planning, okay, what's our roadmap for the next couple quarters?
What are the big projects that we're standing on?
Yeah, no, it makes sense.
How is AEO tweaked how you think about some of that?
Added complexity, you know, sits in there and it's in different ways.
I'm curious how you, how do you balance all that?
Yeah, so from my opinion, or my POV, and I think it's generally Graphite's POV, is that, like, pretty much everything that was necessary for SEO is still necessary for AEO.
So from, like, like, a technical perspective.
If ChatGPT's bots are, or ChatGPT bot is crawling exactly the same way Googlebot is.
I actually saw yesterday with, I think ChatGPT's bot for Atlas is crawling as Googlebot.
Wait, really?
Yeah, they named their user agent Googlebot.
So they have to try to circumvent, maybe?
Like, I know, like, Cloudflare has auto-blocked them in some places.
Yeah, it probably is because Googlebot, you know, is accepted everywhere.
So it's literally crawling exactly the same.
So all the same principles apply in terms of being able to find our content, like, crawlability, indexability of our content.
I think what does change, the on-page, like, we're testing.
So, like, we've found, like, changing headers as questions can be impactful.
Table of contents with jump links still seems like it's impactful.
FAQs are obviously impactful.
We're thinking, like, more...
What are more of the ways that we can get the questions that people are asking in an AI search on the page, similar to just how we would with keywords or topics?
What a few things that are additive, and this is more like long-term strategy, but is we've historically just been focused on owned media.
The earned media is definitely something that's new that we're fully leaning into.

So, and I'm sure you're already aware of all this, but just the importance to identify the citations that are showing up for your prompts that are gearing your brand mentions and earning mentions in those citations.
So we've been standing stuff up internally, like getting messy and like getting freelancers from Upwork to help us run crawls or put prompts in the chat GPT and pull the citations.
We've built two tools internally that are using the API to pull from chat GPT.
We know it's a lot different than if you're actually logged in and going into Fathom.
That's the challenge.
That's why we're using the Upworkers as well.
We've been working with, I guess I can say Noble.
They're like an AI-powered SaaS company.
We met them through Webflow.
We've been testing them with a few clients.
They're like trying to automate the citation analysis and outreach and all of that.
We're a little early to show results.
We're just now in the stages where we're leaning into the outreach.
It's been slow going because we've, A, had to build new partnerships with partnership teams and comm teams.

And they, at least for the clients that I've interacted with so far, they've historically moved very slow where they're reaching out to a few each month and actually trying to build affiliate partnerships and long-term relations.
Whereas like ChatGBT is pulling from so many citations that aren't even like ranking on Google.
Or I'm sure they're ranking, but not ranking high in games.
That is almost more important just to have widespread coverage, at least right now, as opposed to targeting on like those big tech radar, you know, level publishers, tier one publishers.
So that's what we're what we're testing.
And then we're also doing we're doing Reddit strategies as well, which, again, a little early to show AEO data, but the community responses have actually been really good.
And we're taking a fully white hat approach.
So we're not trying to scale scale, you know, Reddit activity with with bots or fake users or anything.

We're actually just empowering internal teams to how to engage with their audiences and then using this our citations analysis to to let to surface them relevant threads that are actually that we believe are impactful for AEO.
So your focus is on.
Yeah, it's funny.
I was like thinking about that in a very similar way.
We don't really do a ton of that, but have experimented a little bit and had a similar kind of idea in the back of my mind.

And so it's more like an enablement angle you think of it as of like rather than you're not the one going in and doing the responses because they have just so much more context and it makes more sense to have that right, you know, expert being able to go have those conversations essentially.
Yeah, exactly.

makes more sense for, because typically they're quite, we're more focused on so far Reddit threads that are specifically discussing the brand, not so much like if we're talking about Webflow, like going on to the web design subreddit and just trying to seed, you know, Webflow into different threads.

The other, there's other on-site or owned media aspect that's different, like most of my career I've been focused on non-brand, because that's where you can get the growth, find new users that aren't familiar with the brand already.

The branded conversation is obviously a lot more important now, especially on the domain, because people are asking, long detail seems to be a lot longer on ChatGPT, so people are asking a lot more questions about the product, about the features, about your competitors.
So we want to have all that on the website, whether it's in the Help Center, whether it's an FAQ page, whatever it is.

So we're helping clients identify, like, what are the questions people are actually asking about your brand, and then creating strategies to answer those, where in the past we would have said, like, the branded content is not so important.

I think the most difficult part is when you're like having a conversation with a client who doesn't have the context or previous knowledge.

Can you describe a time when, like, you had a client strategy or a key deliverable, whatever it may be, that was off track?
You know, what did you do?
Yeah, so I would say earlier in my career, like, it would happen.

But I think even now, like, even if we're doing everything we can to make sure our new buzzword for, like, past year has been seven star, which to me is, like, we were already doing premium above and beyond.
It's just a new way to say it.
But it does resonate with especially newer hires and things like that.
Even if we're doing everything we can to make sure our deliverable is seven stars, there's always going to be misalignment at times.
So I'm just transparent and, like, lean into what the client is actually looking for.
What's helped me is a lot of the framing.
We want to be confident and authoritative.
But a lot of times when we're doing when we're giving deliverables, I'll do a lot of framing of here's the why.
We have what we have so far, but I want to have this conversation with you to make sure this is actually going to ladder into your goals and actually provide value and be actionable for you.
So I'll have that conversation typically before we start the deliverable to help us get aligned.

But that conversation is important when typically we're delivering things live, but we can do it, you know, we can do the same thing async, is frame that to say like, hey, this is our starting port, but we actually need to collaborate in order to make sure this is going to drive the best impact.
And it doesn't, that doesn't mean we're asking the client to like do more work.
It just means we want to make sure we're a team and we have the same vision and we're aligned.
So that's typically, and you know, if you have that mindset, you build that relationship, then the points when you do have the misalignment, it becomes really easy to cover.
And it's not like, oh , I messed up this, this, they're going to hate me now.
It's, it's just a no, let's, let's continue to talk about it and get to where we need to be together.
You know, it can get a little, if there's a timeline, then.
Then it can get hairy, but that's just when you've got to double down and make sure things get done when they need to get done.
